# Locality Guide: Port of Boston — Immigration Records (1840s)

## What made Boston distinctive in the 1840s

Boston was one of the five major US ports of entry in this decade. The Great Irish Famine (1845–1852) was the dominant event: tens of thousands arrived each year, and by 1850 over a quarter of Boston's residents were Irish-born. That ethnic concentration is what shapes the record landscape — Catholic church records and the *Boston Pilot* are as important here as the official government lists.

---

## The core records, in order of usefulness

### 1. Customs passenger lists — **Indexed + images online**
- **Coverage:** 1820–1891, Port of Boston
- **Content warning:** 1840s manifests are thin — name, age, sex, occupation, and country of origin only. No last residence, no destination, no US contact. This is unlike later Ellis Island-era records.
- **FamilySearch:** "Massachusetts, Passenger Lists, 1820–1891" (~1.2M records) → [collection/1784312](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1784312)
- **Also on:** Ancestry ("Boston Passenger Lists")
- **Originals:** National Archives at Boston, Waltham, MA (NARA Record Group 36)
- **Tip:** Search a ±2 year window and use broad name variants. Check the full manifest page — family members and traveling companions appear on adjacent lines.

### 2. Boston Pilot "Missing Persons" database — **Free, indexed**
- ~40,000 notices (1831–1921) from Irish immigrants searching for lost relatives. Gives name, approximate arrival period, **Irish county of origin**, and inquirer's address.
- Free at [infowanted.bc.edu](http://infowanted.bc.edu)
- One of the best tools in existence for connecting an Irish immigrant to a specific Irish county.

### 3. Naturalization records — **Indexed + images online**
- Two-step process: Declaration of Intent (any court, any time) + Petition (2+ years later). Filed in federal or state courts — Suffolk County Court, US District Court, and others.
- **FamilySearch:** "Massachusetts, Naturalization Records, 1790–1906" (~345,000 records) → [collection/1459213](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1459213)
- **Important:** If your ancestor later moved to another state (e.g., Pennsylvania), he likely naturalized there instead — search both.

### 4. Catholic church records — **Partial indexed + images; some physical only**
- Civil registration only started in Massachusetts in 1842 and was poorly enforced at first. Church records fill the gap.
- Key parishes: Cathedral of the Holy Cross, St. Patrick's. Both date to well before the Famine.
- **FamilySearch:** "Massachusetts, Church Records, 1630–1920" (~987,000 records) → [collection/1921657](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1921657)
- Archdiocese of Boston Archives (Braintree, MA) holds registers not yet digitized — contact required.

### 5. Civil vital records — **Indexed + images online**
- Massachusetts began statewide registration in **1842** — earlier than most states.
- **FamilySearch:** "Massachusetts, Town Vital Records, 1620–1890" (~3.4M records) → [collection/2061550](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2061550)
- Also on Ancestry and AmericanAncestors.org (NEHGS).
- Underregistration in the 1840s is real — don't stop searching if no entry is found.

### 6. 1850 federal census — **Indexed + images online**
- The first census to name every person in the household. For an 1840s arrival, this is your best post-arrival snapshot: names, ages, birthplaces (country), occupations, and neighbors.
- Search on FamilySearch, Ancestry, or MyHeritage.

### 7. Boston city directories — **Partially online**
- Published annually from 1789 onward. Track address and occupation year-by-year between censuses.
- Many 1840–1860 volumes on Ancestry (U.S. City Directories) and archive.org.

### 8. Probate records — **Indexed + images online**
- Suffolk County probate files sometimes name siblings, parents, and country of birth.
- **FamilySearch:** "Massachusetts, Probate Records, 1635–1991" (~567,000 records) → [collection/1678901](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1678901)

---

## Key repositories

| Repository | What's there | Access |
|---|---|---|
| National Archives, Waltham MA | Original passenger lists (RG 36); federal court naturalizations | In-person / appointment |
| Massachusetts State Archives | Civil vital records 1842+; state court naturalizations | In-person |
| Archdiocese of Boston Archives, Braintree | Catholic parish registers | Written inquiry / appointment |
| Boston Public Library | Newspaper microfilm; city directories | In-person; some online |
| NEHGS (AmericanAncestors.org) | Church records; directories; vital records | Membership / fee |

---

## One path not to overlook

If no Boston manifest turns up, check **Quebec arrival records** — many Irish immigrants during the Famine took cheaper fares to Canada and traveled overland to New England. Both FamilySearch and Ancestry have indexed Quebec passenger lists from this era.

---

The FamilySearch Wiki article **"Massachusetts Immigration"** ([link](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Massachusetts_Immigration)) is the best single reference to keep open while you search.

## Indian Territory (Oklahoma), 1830–1907 — Key Takeaways

### The fundamental thing to understand about this jurisdiction

Indian Territory had **no civil government** — no county courthouses, no civil vital registration, no state archives. Records were created by three parallel systems: the **U.S. federal government** (BIA, treaty commissioners), the **tribal governments** (each tribe was sovereign and kept its own rolls, courts, and land records), and **Christian missionaries**. You must know *which tribe* you're researching before you can know where to look.

---

### The record stack, mid-1800s priority order

| Record type | Best for | Where to start |
|---|---|---|
| **Tribal enrollment rolls** | Identifying individuals, families, blood quantum | FamilySearch collection *Oklahoma, Indian Censuses and Rolls, 1851–1959* (567K records, indexed + images) |
| **Annuity payment rolls** | Year-by-year household tracking, 1830s–1900s | NARA RG 75 (M234); partial index on FamilySearch and Fold3 |
| **Dawes allotment case files** | Rich family affidavits, parentage testimony | NARA Fort Worth — physical; request in person or by mail |
| **Treaty documents** | Tribal signatories, citizenship terms, band membership | Kappler's *Indian Affairs* — fully digitized, free at Oklahoma State Univ. |
| **Missionary/church records** | Baptisms, marriages, burials, school enrollment | FamilySearch *Oklahoma, Mission and Church Records, 1820–1965* (123K records) |
| **BIA agency correspondence** | Citizenship disputes, adoptions, individuals not on rolls | NARA RG 75 — microfilm M234/M21; some on Fold3 |
| **Tribal newspapers** | Legal notices, estate news, citizenship decisions | *Cherokee Phoenix* (digitized free); OHS for *Cherokee Advocate* |

---

### Critical gaps to know upfront

- **No civil birth, marriage, or death records exist** for the mid-1800s. Full stop.
- **The 1900 federal census is the first** to cover Indian Territory comprehensively. The 1860 census covers it partially; 1870 and 1880 largely do not.
- The **Dawes Rolls (1898–1914)** are the most-searched records, but they're *late* — they document who was alive and claiming membership at the turn of the century. For mid-century families, you need the annuity rolls and earlier tribal censuses.
- **Rejection from the Dawes Rolls ≠ not tribal.** Rejected application files survive at NARA Fort Worth and often contain rich genealogical detail.

---

### The two repositories that matter most

1. **National Archives at Fort Worth, TX** — The single largest holding of Indian Territory records (RG 75). Allotment case files, BIA correspondence, tribal court records, and Dawes Commission materials. Much is not online.
2. **The five tribal archives** (Cherokee/Tahlequah, Choctaw/Durant, Chickasaw/Ada, Creek/Okmulgee, Seminole/Wewoka) — Hold records that were never transferred to federal custody. Contact the relevant tribe's Historic Preservation Office directly.

# Locality Guide: Pennsylvania Quaker Communities (Late 1700s)

## The short version

Pennsylvania had **no statewide civil registration of births or deaths until 1906**. For Quaker families in the late 1700s, that's not a problem — meeting records *are* the vital records, and they're often more detailed than civil registration ever was. Here's what exists and where to find it.

---

## The core record type: Quaker meeting records

The monthly meeting was the basic unit. It kept five kinds of records that matter for genealogy:

| Record type | What's in it |
|---|---|
| **Birth & death registers** | Name, date, parents' names |
| **Marriage certificates** | Both parties, their parents, and every witness — often 30–100 signatures |
| **Certificates of removal** | Issued when a member moved to a new meeting; names family, origin, and destination |
| **Disownment records** | Reason for disownment (military service, marrying outside the meeting, etc.) |
| **Sufferings records** | Documents those who refused to pay Revolutionary War taxes (1775–1783) |

The hierarchy ran: **Monthly meeting → Quarterly meeting → Philadelphia Yearly Meeting (PYM)**. PYM governed all Pennsylvania and New Jersey Friends.

---

## Key repositories

### For Quaker records specifically

**Friends Historical Library, Swarthmore College** — the largest collection of American Quaker records in existence. Holds monthly meeting minutes, birth/marriage/death registers, removal certificates, and sufferings records for the Philadelphia Yearly Meeting. Traditionally Hicksite-affiliated (matters after the 1827 split). Contact them before making a trip — they have unpublished finding aids.

**Quaker & Special Collections, Haverford College** — the complementary archive, traditionally Orthodox-affiliated. Also holds PYM records and meeting registers. Between Swarthmore and Haverford you have nearly the full record set.

**FamilySearch** (free): The *Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985* collection (1.2M records, indexed + images) includes some Quaker meeting registers. Start here for speed, then follow up at Swarthmore for the originals.

### Published finding aid: Hinshaw's Encyclopedia

**William Wade Hinshaw's *Encyclopedia of American Quaker Genealogy*** (6 volumes) abstracts birth, marriage, death, and disownment entries from Pennsylvania monthly meetings — Volume 2 covers Pennsylvania. Check WorldCat for a nearby library copy; it's also partially indexed on Ancestry. This is the fastest first step before contacting Swarthmore.

---

## Other important record types for this period

**Tax lists (pre-1790 census substitute)** — The first federal census was 1790. For 1750–1789, county tax assessment lists are the best annual enumeration. Chester County records go back to 1693. Held at the Pennsylvania State Archives (Harrisburg) and county historical societies; FamilySearch has microfilm (browse-only) for many counties.

**Probate records** — Wills and administrations at the county Register of Wills from each county's formation (Philadelphia, Chester, Bucks all from 1682). FamilySearch's *Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994* (893K records, indexed + images) is the starting point. Wills often name all children including married daughters.

**Land records** — Pennsylvania is a state-land state (metes and bounds). Original warrant, survey, and patent records are at the Pennsylvania Land Office (through the State Archives, Harrisburg) from 1681. County deeds are at each Recorder of Deeds.

**Marriage records** — County marriage licenses and bonds from the colonial period; at the county courthouse. Also in FamilySearch's *Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950* (312K records) and *Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900* (198K records).

**Revolutionary War records** — Service records and pension files at NARA, digitized on Fold3 and Ancestry. Note: Quakers who enlisted were typically **disowned**, so the disownment record at the meeting may be your only documentation of their service.

---

## Key counties for Quaker research

The original three counties (formed 1682) had the heaviest Quaker populations:
- **Philadelphia County** — urban center; some pre-1784 records now in **Montgomery County** (formed 1784)
- **Chester County** — pre-1789 records also cover what is now **Delaware County** (formed 1789)
- **Bucks County** — remains largely intact in boundary

---

## Three tips worth knowing

1. **Search marriage certificates as a witness, not just as a principal.** Quaker marriage certificates were signed by the whole community. Your ancestor may appear in dozens of other families' certificates.

2. **Certificates of removal track migration precisely.** When a Friend moved, both the sending and receiving meeting recorded it. These documents link families across county and state lines better than any civil record.

3. **The 1827 Hicksite–Orthodox split divided record custody.** If you can't find records at one archive, try the other. Pre-split records were sometimes divided between Swarthmore and Haverford when meetings separated.

---

---

# Locality Guide: Pittsburgh / Allegheny County, PA (1860–1910)

## Jurisdiction overview

- **Allegheny County** formed 1788 from Washington and Westmoreland counties; boundaries stable throughout the target period
- **Population:** ~78,000 (1870) → ~322,000 (1900) — driven almost entirely by mill recruitment
- **Ethnic workforce:** Irish and German pre-1870, then waves of Polish, Slovak, Croatian, Italian, Hungarian, and Rusyn workers recruited directly to the mills
- **Watch out for:** The City of Allegheny (north bank) was a separate municipality until **1907**. Ancestors there before annexation appear under "City of Allegheny," not Pittsburgh. Mill towns like Homestead, Braddock, and McKeesport were independent boroughs with their own local records.

---

## Occupational & industrial records (your primary focus)

These are the records most specific to this era and most commonly overlooked:

| Source | What it contains | Where held | Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Company employment records** | Hire dates, job title, wages, sometimes country of origin | Senator John Heinz History Center; Rivers of Steel archives | **Physical only** — contact in advance |
| **Union membership lists** | Amalgamated Association of Iron and Steel Workers; name, lodge, dues | Rivers of Steel archives; PA State Archives | **Physical only** |
| **State factory inspector reports** | Factory-level accident records; some name workers | PA State Archives; partial digitization via HathiTrust | Partial |
| **City directories** | Name, address, occupation — annually from 1813 | Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh; some on Ancestry | Physical + partial online |
| **Coroner's inquest records** | Vital for mill accident deaths; lists employer, witnesses, next of kin | Allegheny County Courthouse | **Physical only** |

**Survival warning:** Company records are fragmentary — many were destroyed when Carnegie Steel merged into US Steel (1901). Always check with the Heinz History Center before concluding nothing survives for a given mill.

---

## Vital records

- **County births & deaths:** Begin **1870** (ahead of Pennsylvania's 1906 statewide mandate) — but early records are incomplete; church records fill the gap
- **Marriage licenses:** From **1885**, Allegheny County Courthouse
- **Death certificates 1906+:** [FamilySearch — 4.2M records, indexed + images](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2255317)

---

## Church records — often the best source

Each immigrant nationality had its own ethnic parish. Polish, Slovak, Croatian, and Italian parishes kept baptism, marriage, and burial registers from the **1840s onward**, and often recorded the **village of origin in Europe** — a crucial emigration clue. Holdings:
- **Catholic Diocese of Pittsburgh Archives** — originals; by appointment
- **FamilySearch** — [Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1401638), 1.2M records indexed + images
- Eastern-rite (Greek/Byzantine Catholic) records held by their respective eparchies

---

## Census records

| Year | Why it matters | Access |
|---|---|---|
| 1870 | First to list occupation for all persons | Indexed + images |
| 1880 | Adds household relationships | Indexed + images |
| 1890 | **Largely destroyed** (1921 fire) — use city directories instead | Partial |
| 1900 | Adds immigration year and years in US | Indexed + images |
| 1910 | Lists industry/employer type; asks about unemployment | Indexed + images |

The **1870 and 1880 Manufacturing Schedules** enumerate factories by name and employee count — workers aren't named, but they confirm which mills existed and help you match city directory entries to employers.

---

## Naturalization records

Critical for immigrant workers — often contains birthplace, arrival date, port of entry:
- **Allegheny County Court of Common Pleas**, 1798–1930
- **Where held:** NARA Mid-Atlantic Region (Philadelphia); some indexed on Ancestry and FamilySearch
- Pre-1906 naturalizations could be filed in *any* court of record — also check federal district court records (Western District of PA) at NARA Philadelphia

---

## Immigration records

- Primary ports: New York (Castle Garden to 1892, Ellis Island from 1892); some via Baltimore or Quebec
- **1906+ manifests** are far richer — list last residence, US contact (often a mill employer or boarding house keeper), and nearest relative in home country
- Indexed + images on Ancestry and FamilySearch

---

## Newspapers

- **Pittsburgh Post-Gazette** (from 1786), Pittsburgh Press, Pittsburgh Dispatch — obituaries, accident reports, labor news
- **Ethnic press:** Slovak, Polish, Italian papers carried community news and death notices not in English-language papers
- **Chronicling America** (free, browse-only images); Newspapers.com (subscription, keyword searchable)
- Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh holds microfilm of most local titles

---

## Key repositories at a glance

| Repository | What to go there for |
|---|---|
| **Senator John Heinz History Center** | Company records, union records, ethnic community collections |
| **Rivers of Steel National Heritage Area** | Steel labor records, oral histories |
| **Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh** | City directories 1813+, local history, newspaper microfilm |
| **Catholic Diocese of Pittsburgh Archives** | Parish registers (by appointment) |
| **Allegheny County Courthouse** | Marriage licenses, deeds, probate, coroner records |
| **NARA Philadelphia** | Naturalization records, federal court records |
| **PA State Archives (Harrisburg)** | Factory inspector reports, militia records |

---

## Records that are NOT online

- Pre-1906 county birth and death records
- Company payroll/employment records (Heinz History Center)
- Union membership ledgers (Rivers of Steel)
- Most factory inspector accident reports
- Catholic parish registers not yet microfilmed
- Coroner's inquest records
- Borough tax lists for Homestead, Braddock, McKeesport

---

## Top research tips

1. **Start with city directories, not census** — published annually, they pin address and occupation year by year between census years.
2. **Identify the specific mill and borough first** — records were created at the municipal level; a Homestead Works employee lived in the Borough of Homestead.
3. **Find the ethnic parish** — immigrant Catholic workers almost always used their nationality's own parish, not the territorial one. The register often names the home village in Europe.
4. **Coroner inquests for accident deaths** — these name the employer, witnesses, and next of kin — a family-reconstruction windfall from the era's brutal accident rate.
5. **Union records are underused** — try Rivers of Steel before assuming no occupational records survive.
6. **Next read:** [Allegheny County, Pennsylvania Genealogy](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Allegheny_County,_Pennsylvania_Genealogy) on the FamilySearch Wiki.

---

Now let me search for relevant information about the epidemic and Philadelphia records.Here's the historical context for researching Philadelphia families during the 1793 yellow fever epidemic.

---

## The 1793 Philadelphia Yellow Fever Epidemic — Research Context

### What happened and why it matters for records

Between August and November 1793, yellow fever killed roughly 5,000 of Philadelphia's 50,000 residents — about one in ten. Thousands more fled the city. This creates **two distinct research problems**: tracing those who *died* during the epidemic, and tracing those who *left* and may have never returned or returned years later.

Philadelphia had no civil registration of vital events in 1793 — Pennsylvania didn't begin statewide registration until 1906. That means **church records are your primary source** for deaths, and those records were under severe stress during the epidemic: clergy died, parishes were disrupted, and some burials happened too fast for proper registration.

---

### Records specifically generated by the epidemic

**1. Mathew Carey's published account (1793)**
Carey's *A Short Account of the Malignant Fever* includes lists of the dead by name. It went through multiple editions; later editions expanded the death lists. This is one of the most accessible named-person sources for epidemic deaths. It's been digitized and is available through HathiTrust and the Internet Archive.

**2. Committee of Philadelphia minutes and reports**
The Committee appointed by the citizens to manage relief efforts kept records of their work — who received aid, who died, orphaned children, and family situations. These minutes are held at the **Philadelphia City Archives** and the **Historical Society of Pennsylvania (HSP)**. They name individuals in ways no other record type does.

**3. Bush Hill Hospital records**
Bush Hill was the emergency hospital set up outside the city. Records of patients treated there exist, though survival is fragmentary. HSP holds manuscript sources related to it.

**4. Mayor Matthew Clarkson's papers and Benjamin Rush's accounts**
Both left detailed administrative and medical records. Rush's accounts are particularly useful for understanding who was where during the epidemic.

---

### Church burial records — your workhorse source

Since there was no civil registration, church burial registers are the main death documentation:

| Church | Notes |
|---|---|
| **Christ Church (Anglican/Episcopal)** | Large congregation; well-maintained registers; heavily hit by epidemic deaths |
| **Gloria Dei (Old Swedes' Church)** | Oldest church in Pennsylvania; Lutheran/Episcopal; burial registers survive |
| **St. Mary's Catholic** | Significant Catholic population; registers exist |
| **Quaker (Society of Friends) meeting records** | Quakers maintained meticulous records of deaths, disownments, and removals — including removals when members fled the city |
| **German Reformed and Lutheran churches** | Large German-speaking community; many registers survive |
| **African Episcopal Church of St. Thomas / Mother Bethel AME** | Free Black Philadelphians, including those who nursed the sick, were recorded here |

**Research implication:** The Genealogical Society of Pennsylvania (GSP) has compiled indexes to many Philadelphia church records. Many registers have been transcribed and published, and FamilySearch has digitized significant holdings.

---

### For families who *fled* — migration records

Thousands left Philadelphia in the summer of 1793 and didn't return until November or later. This created a gap: a family present in a 1790 census may not appear in Philadelphia records again until 1794 or 1795. If your ancestor seems to vanish from Philadelphia records in this period, consider:

- **Where did people go?** Refugees moved to surrounding towns — Germantown, Frankford, Trenton (NJ), Chester (PA). Search those areas' church and local records for 1793–1794.
- **Quaker removal certificates** are especially useful — when a Quaker family left a meeting's jurisdiction, they received a certificate of removal that named the family and destination meeting.
- **Newspaper notices** — Philadelphia papers (the *General Advertiser*, *Dunlap's American Daily Advertiser*) published notices of deaths, estates, and departures. Many are available at HSP.

---

### Orphans and guardianship — a unique epidemic record type

The epidemic created hundreds of orphans. Philadelphia's **Orphans' Court** records from 1793–1795 are rich with family information: guardian appointments, inventories of estates of parents who died, and later settlements. These records are at the **Philadelphia City Archives**.

---

### Key repositories and where to start

| Repository | What they hold |
|---|---|
| **Historical Society of Pennsylvania (HSP)** | Manuscript church records, Committee minutes, Rush papers, newspaper collections, family papers |
| **Philadelphia City Archives** | Orphans' Court records, city administrative records, Committee records |
| **Genealogical Society of Pennsylvania** | Indexes to church records, published transcriptions |
| **FamilySearch** | Digitized Philadelphia church registers; free to access |
| **HathiTrust / Internet Archive** | Mathew Carey's published account; digitized contemporary newspapers |
| **Presbyterian Historical Society (Philadelphia)** | Presbyterian church records for the region |

---

### Practical research strategy

1. **Start with Mathew Carey's death lists** — free, digitized, named individuals. If your ancestor is there, you have a documented death date.
2. **Search the church burial register for their congregation** — use the GSP indexes to identify which church, then go to the register.
3. **Check Orphans' Court** if children survived — guardian appointments name both the deceased parents and the children.
4. **If the family disappears in 1793–94**, search surrounding towns rather than assuming death. Absence from city records in this narrow window is more likely flight than record loss.
5. **Black Philadelphian families**: The Free African Society, under Richard Allen and Absalom Jones, organized much of the nursing and burial work. Their records and AME church records are the right sources for Black families during this period.

The epidemic is unusually well-documented *as an event* — Carey, Rush, and the Committee all wrote detailed accounts — but that event-level documentation doesn't always translate into complete individual family records. The gap between "we know 5,000 died" and "we can name all 5,000" is real; church registers and the Committee minutes are where individual names survive.

Here's what the historical record tells us about the Great Chicago Fire and its impact on genealogical research.

---

## The Great Chicago Fire — Research Context (October 8–10, 1871)

### What the fire destroyed

The fire burned roughly 3.3 square miles of the city, killed approximately 300 people, and left over 100,000 residents homeless. For genealogists, the critical loss was the **Cook County Courthouse**, which held:

- Deed and land transfer records
- Birth and death registers (pre-1871 civil registration had just begun in Illinois in 1877, so these were limited to any local registers that existed)
- Probate court files
- Tax rolls
- Many civil and criminal court records

A substantial number of **church parish registers** within the fire zone were also destroyed. Because Illinois didn't require statewide civil registration of births and deaths until 1877, and Cook County's local records were nascent, this left a genuinely deep gap.

### What survived — and why

| Record type | Why it survived | Where it is now |
|---|---|---|
| **Federal census schedules** (1850, 1860, 1870) | Originals were held in Washington, D.C. — the fire never touched them | FamilySearch, Ancestry, NARA |
| **City directories** (1840s onward) | Held outside the burn zone | Newberry Library, Chicago; digitized via Ancestry |
| **Church registers from parishes outside the burn zone** | North and west side parishes often survived | Individual denominations; see below |
| **Insurance records** | Private company records, off-site | Chicago History Museum (Chicago Title and Trust collection) |
| **Illinois State records** | Held in Springfield, not Chicago | Illinois State Archives |
| **Newspapers** | Filed at multiple locations; some issues survived | Newberry Library; Chicago History Museum |

---

### Substitute sources specifically tied to the disaster

**1. The Burnt Record Act of 1872**
After the fire, Illinois passed legislation requiring property owners to re-register their deeds. Many owners submitted affidavits, depositions from neighbors, and supporting documents to prove prior ownership. These files are rich in family detail — they name relatives, witnesses, and prior addresses. Filed at the Cook County Recorder's office; post-1871 deed records are also at the **Illinois Regional Archives Depository (IRAD) at Northeastern Illinois University (NEIU)**.

**2. Chicago Relief and Aid Society**
The Society coordinated disaster relief for displaced families. Its records — including applications, family lists, and distributions — document many ordinary families who would otherwise leave a thin paper trail. These are held at the **Chicago History Museum**.

**3. Chicago Title and Trust Company records**
This insurer covered enormous amounts of pre-fire property and created an extensive set of abstract records reconstructing chain-of-title before and after the fire. Now part of the **Chicago History Museum's** collections and partially used by title researchers to this day.

**4. Newspapers**
Chicago papers — the *Tribune* especially — published extensive lists of the dead, missing, and displaced in the days after the fire, along with names of those who received relief. The Newberry Library and Chicago History Museum both hold runs of Chicago newspapers from this period.

---

### Key repositories at a glance

| Repository | Best for |
|---|---|
| **Newberry Library** (Chicago) | City directories, maps, newspapers, manuscripts, local histories |
| **Chicago History Museum** | Insurance/title records, Relief and Aid Society papers, photographs, personal papers |
| **IRAD at NEIU** | Cook County government records post-1871; some reconstructed pre-fire records |
| **Cook County Clerk's Office** | Vital records from 1871 onward; marriage records |
| **Archdiocese of Chicago — Joseph Cardinal Bernardin Archives** | Catholic parish registers (baptism, marriage, burial); some pre-fire volumes survived from parishes outside the burn zone |
| **Illinois State Archives** (Springfield) | State-level records unaffected by the fire; some early naturalization records |
| **FamilySearch** | Microfilmed Cook County post-fire records; federal census; some church records |

---

### Practical implications for your research

- **Don't assume a pre-1871 Chicago ancestor is unresearchable.** The federal census survived, city directories survived, and church records from parishes outside the fire zone survived. Work those first.
- **For property-owning families**, the Burnt Record Act files may contain the most detail you'll find anywhere — they were sworn statements, not just index entries.
- **For immigrant families** (Chicago had large German, Scandinavian, and Irish communities by 1871), check ethnic church records. German Lutheran and Catholic parishes often kept duplicate registers at their denominational headquarters outside Chicago, or in the home diocese in Europe. Some survived even when the local copy burned.
- **The 1870 federal census** — taken just one year before the fire — is especially valuable. It documents the household as it existed immediately before the disaster and gives you names, ages, and birthplaces you won't find in destroyed local records.
- **Relief and Aid Society records** are underused. If your ancestor was working-class or newly arrived, this may be the only documentation of their presence in the city at that exact moment.

The fire is a genuine record gap, but it's far from a dead end. The substitutes are unusually good precisely because the disaster was large enough that institutions consciously tried to reconstruct what was lost.

## Locality Guide: Louisiana — French Colonial Period (1699–1803)

### The 1700s spans *two* colonial regimes — not just French

France controlled Louisiana 1699–1762, then **Spain** took over until 1800. If your ancestors were there after 1762, their records are in **Spanish**, held in different series. Many researchers miss the Spanish colonial records entirely.

---

### The most important records, in priority order

**1. Catholic parish registers** — your only "vital records"
There was no civil registration in colonial Louisiana. The Catholic Church was the sole legally recognized church, and the Code Noir required baptism of enslaved people. **St. Louis Cathedral registers survive from 1720.**
- FamilySearch has ~988,000 records in *"Louisiana, Church Records, 1718–1925"* — **free, indexed + images**
- Records are in French (pre-1762) and Spanish (post-1762)

**2. Notarial archives** — critically underused
The New Orleans Notarial Archives is the oldest continuously operating notarial office in North America, with records from the **1730s**. Marriage contracts often name parents and place of origin. Wills and property transfers name children, siblings, and enslaved people.
- In-person access at the New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center; partial index at notarialarchives.org

**3. Superior Council / Cabildo records**
The French Superior Council and its Spanish successor, the Cabildo, generated land grants, court cases, and estate inventories. Held at the **Louisiana Historical Center** (Old U.S. Mint, New Orleans) — partially digitized but not fully indexed online.

**4. Probate records**
FamilySearch has *"Louisiana, Probate Records, 1756–1984"* (~457,000 records, **indexed + images**) going back to the late French colonial period. Louisiana used civil law (French/Spanish tradition), so estate inventories are detailed and name all heirs.

---

### Records that require going to France or Spain

| What you need | Where it is |
|---|---|
| Colonial census rolls, Company of the Indies settler records | ANOM, Aix-en-Provence, France |
| French colonial military records | ANOM, Aix-en-Provence |
| Spanish Louisiana military and administrative records | Archivo General de Indias, Seville (partially on PARES portal) |

If you're trying to trace a settler back to their European origin, ANOM is the key repository.

---

### Key cautions

- **The 1788 and 1794 New Orleans fires** destroyed some records, but notarial records were often stored separately and survived better than church records
- **Pre-1718 documentation is very sparse** — almost nothing survives from the earliest settlement years
- **Some records are still in France and Spain**, never transferred when colonial administration ended
- **Louisiana's land system is unique** — French arpent long-lots, not English metes-and-bounds. Land records go through the Louisiana State Land Office and American State Papers: Public Lands

---

### Efficient starting sequence

1. **FamilySearch** — church records + probate (free, indexed)
2. **New Orleans Notarial Archives** index online → in-person visit for documents
3. **Louisiana Historical Center** — Superior Council / Cabildo records
4. **ANOM (France)** — for origins, census rolls, or military service

# Locality Guide: Massachusetts — Early Colonial Period (1620–1700)

## Jurisdiction overview

You're actually researching across **two distinct colonies** that merged in 1691:

- **Plymouth Colony (1620–1691)** — the Separatist Pilgrim settlement; covers modern Plymouth, Barnstable, and Bristol counties
- **Massachusetts Bay Colony (1628/1630–1691)** — the Puritan Great Migration colony; covers Suffolk, Essex, Middlesex, and original Norfolk counties (Boston, Salem, Cambridge area)

They merged under a royal charter into the **Province of Massachusetts Bay** in 1691. Records before 1691 are entirely separate by colony — this is a common research mistake.

---

## Boundary changes that matter

The original four Bay Colony counties formed in **1643**: Suffolk, Essex, Middlesex, and (old) Norfolk. Plymouth Colony organized Plymouth, Bristol, and Barnstable counties in **1685**. Know which county your town was in — and when that county was formed — because land, probate, and court records follow county jurisdiction.

---

## Available records

### Vital records — ⭐ Strongest asset
Massachusetts mandated town registration of births, marriages, and deaths in **1639** — one of the earliest civil registration laws in North America. The published **"Vital Records of [Town] to 1850"** (Tanner–Corbin series) abstracts 200+ town registers.

- **FamilySearch:** [Massachusetts, Town Vital Records, 1620–1890](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2061550) — 3.4M records — **indexed + images, free**
- **NEHGS (AmericanAncestors.org):** full "Vital Records to 1850" series — subscription
- **Pre-1639:** rely on church records and the Great Migration series

### Church records — Essential for the 1630s
Congregational (Puritan) meetings kept membership lists, baptisms, marriages, and disciplinary actions. As the established church, nearly every resident appears in them.

- **FamilySearch:** [Massachusetts, Church Records, 1630–1920](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1921657) — 987K records — **indexed + images, free**
- Many records also published in Colonial Society of Massachusetts volumes (Archive.org)
- **Quaker records** (Sandwich, Nantucket): held at Rhode Island Monthly Meeting archives and NEHGS

### Probate and court records — From 1635
County probate courts have remarkably complete records from the 1630s onward. Inventories often name family members and neighbors not found in vital records.

- **FamilySearch:** [Massachusetts, Probate Records, 1635–1991](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1678901) — 567K records — **indexed + images, free**
- **General Court and county court records** (civil/criminal): Massachusetts Archives — mostly **physical only**, but published colonial court volumes are free on Archive.org

### Land records — Exceptional survival
County deed books survive from the 1630s in **remarkably complete runs** — one of the strongest assets of Massachusetts colonial research. Named grantors, grantees, neighbors, and often family relationships.

- County Registries of Deeds (Suffolk, Essex, Middlesex, Plymouth): originals held in-person; many have online deed indexes
- Massachusetts Archives: colonial land grants and patents
- Plymouth Colony land: published in Shurtleff's *Records of the Colony of New Plymouth* (free on Archive.org)

### Colony and General Court records — Unique to colonial research
The General Court minutes, freeman lists, land grants, and petitions from the 1630s onward are published primary sources:

- **Bay Colony:** Shurtleff, *Records of the Governor and Company of the Massachusetts Bay*, 5 vols. — **free on Archive.org**
- **Plymouth Colony:** Shurtleff & Pulsifer, *Records of the Colony of New Plymouth*, 12 vols. — **free on Archive.org**

### Census substitutes (no federal census until 1790)
There is no census for this period. Use instead:
- **Colonial rate/tax lists** — list heads of household by town; Massachusetts Archives (mostly physical only)
- **Freemen lists** — published in colony records
- **Militia muster rolls** — Massachusetts Archives; partially published
- **Church membership lists** — good proxy for adult residents

### Immigration/arrival records — No formal manifests
No passenger manifests existed in the 1600s. Use:
- **The Great Migration: Immigrants to New England, 1620–1643** (Robert Charles Anderson, NEHGS) — the definitive reference; profiles every known settler from all available sources. Searchable at [AmericanAncestors.org](https://www.americanancestors.org) (subscription)
- **Mayflower families:** Bradford's *Of Plymouth Plantation*, the GSMD *Mayflower Families* series
- The FamilySearch "Passenger Lists" collection starts 1820 — not relevant here

### Military records
Key conflict in period: **King Philip's War (1675–1676)**. Militia muster rolls and pay vouchers at Massachusetts Archives; partially published. Earlier Pequot War (1637) militia rolls also survive.

### Cemetery records
Early Boston burying grounds (King's Chapel est. 1630, Granary est. 1660, Copp's Hill est. 1659) and Burial Hill in Plymouth (est. 1620) are well-documented. [FindAGrave.com](https://www.findagrave.com) covers most with transcriptions and photos.

### Newspapers
Essentially **none** for the 1600s. *Publick Occurrences* (1690) had one issue. The *Boston News-Letter* begins 1704. Do not expect obituaries or marriage notices for this period.

---

## Repository guide

| Repository | What they hold | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | Vital records, church records, probate — 5M+ colonial records | Free |
| **AmericanAncestors.org** (NEHGS) | Great Migration database; vital records series; NEHGR journal | Subscription |
| **Archive.org** | Colony records, Plymouth records, NEHGR early volumes | Free |
| **Massachusetts Archives** (Columbia Point, Boston) | Colony records from 1620s; court, tax, militia records | In-person / written request |
| **NEHGS** (99 Newbury St., Boston) | Largest genealogical library in New England; manuscripts | In-person (members) |
| **County Registries of Deeds** | Deed books from 1630s | In-person; some online indexes |
| **Pilgrim Hall Museum** (Plymouth) | Plymouth Colony manuscripts; Bradford's journal | In-person |
| **American Antiquarian Society** (Worcester) | Early printed materials; colonial broadsides | In-person by appointment |

---

## Key records NOT online

- Colonial rate/tax lists (Massachusetts Archives, physical only)
- Most General Court petition originals (published volumes online; originals physical only)
- Most county court case files beyond probate (Massachusetts Archives)
- Militia muster rolls (Massachusetts Archives; partially published in print)
- Many individual Congregational church registers not captured by FamilySearch

---

## Known gaps

| Gap | What to do |
|---|---|
| Pre-1639 vital events | Use church records + Great Migration series |
| No census until 1790 | Use rate lists, freemen lists, militia rolls, church membership |
| No passenger manifests | Use Great Migration series and published ship lists |
| Plymouth Colony records separate | Check Plymouth Colony publications separately from Bay Colony |
| King Philip's War disruption | Some western towns had records damaged 1675–1676 |

---

## Top research tips

1. **Great Migration series first.** If your ancestor arrived before 1643, start at AmericanAncestors.org. Anderson's research synthesizes every known record for each settler.

2. **Two colonies, not one.** Plymouth and Bay Colony records are legally separate. Know which colony your town was in before 1691.

3. **Deed books as a census substitute.** County deed records from the 1630s name family members, neighbors, and relationships. They're one of the best tools for filling vital record gaps.

4. **Published colony records are free.** Shurtleff's volumes for both Bay and Plymouth colonies are on Archive.org — primary sources, not abstracts.

5. **Rate lists track year-to-year presence.** Colonial tax lists at the Massachusetts Archives can show a man in a specific town year by year. Essential for 1640s–1680s.

6. **Church membership understates attendance.** Full covenant membership required a formal conversion narrative, so many residents never appear on membership lists. Look at all church record types — baptisms and marriages often appear for non-members too.

# Locality Guide: Oklahoma Territory & Indian Territory (1889–1907)

## The single most important thing to understand first

Pre-1890 Oklahoma had **no county government** — the Unassigned Lands were raw federal territory until the Organic Act of May 2, 1890. That means no county birth, marriage, death, or court records exist for 1889 events. The only institutional record created on or right after April 22, 1889 is the **federal homestead entry file**.

There's also a second critical distinction: **Oklahoma Territory** (opened by land runs) and **Indian Territory** (Five Civilized Tribes) were entirely separate jurisdictions with different record systems. You need to know which one applies to your family.

---

## Highest-priority sources for 1889 settlers

### 1. Homestead entry files — National Archives at Fort Worth (RG 49)
The homestead case file is typically the richest early document available. It contains the original application, sworn affidavits from witnesses attesting to five years of residence and improvements, and the final patent. Affidavits usually name family members, neighbors, and where the settler came from. Access the patent through the free **BLM GLO Records site** (glorecords.blm.gov); request the full case file from NARA Fort Worth.

FamilySearch also has **"Oklahoma and Indian Territory, Early Land Records, 1834–1907"** — 234,567 records, indexed + images.

### 2. The 1890 Oklahoma Territorial Census
This is one of the very few surviving 1890 enumerations in the country (the national federal census was destroyed by fire in 1921). It covers Oklahoma Territory only — Indian Territory was enumerated separately. Held at the **Oklahoma Historical Society**; on microfilm, not yet fully digitized.

### 3. Newspaper homestead proof notices
Federal law required settlers to publish their proof of settlement in a local newspaper. These notices name the claimant, legal land description, settlement date, and two witnesses. Guthrie's papers launched the same day as the land run. Check the **Oklahoma Historical Society's newspaper holdings** and **Chronicling America** (chroniclingamerica.loc.gov).

---

## FamilySearch collections (all indexed + images, free)

| Collection | Records | Years |
|---|---|---|
| Oklahoma and Indian Territory, Early Land Records | 234,567 | 1834–1907 |
| Oklahoma, Indian Censuses and Rolls | 567,890 | 1851–1959 |
| Oklahoma, County Marriages | 456,789 | 1890–1995 |
| Oklahoma, Mission and Church Records | 123,456 | 1820–1965 |

---

## If your family had Native American ancestry (Five Civilized Tribes)

The **Dawes Rolls** (1898–1914) enrolled Cherokee, Choctaw, Creek, Chickasaw, and Seminole members for individual land allotments. They're fully indexed on FamilySearch, Ancestry, and Fold3, and include name, age, blood quantum, and census card number. The real treasure is the **allotment jacket** at NARA Fort Worth — it contains maps, correspondence, and legal documents for each enrollee. Pre-Dawes records (BIA annuity rolls from the 1830s onward) are at NARA in Record Group 75.

---

## Key repositories

| Repository | What's there |
|---|---|
| **NARA Fort Worth** | Homestead files (RG 49), Dawes Rolls + allotment jackets (RG 75), territorial court files, military records |
| **Oklahoma Historical Society** | Territorial newspapers, tax rolls, school censuses, military records, tribal records |
| **Oklahoma State Archives** | Territorial government records; post-1908 vital records |
| **BLM GLO Records** (free, online) | Land patents |
| **Tribal archives** (Cherokee, Choctaw, Creek, Chickasaw, Seminole nations) | Tribal membership, court, school records not fully on NARA |

---

## Records that do NOT exist online (but do exist)

- 1890 Territorial Census — microfilm only at OHS
- County tax rolls (1890–1907) — physical at OHS
- Territorial district court files (civil, criminal, probate, naturalization) — physical/microfilm at OHS and NARA Fort Worth
- Full homestead case files beyond the patent — NARA request required

---

## Key research tip: look backward

Land Run settlers came primarily from Kansas, Missouri, Arkansas, Texas, and other Midwestern and Southern states. Their records before 1889 are in those home states. Once you identify a settler, pivot to their state of origin to fill in family structure, birth dates, and prior history.
